content.text: Throughput measures how much work an LLM serving system completes per unit of time. Unlike latency, which tracks an individual request's experience, throughput characterises the aggregate system capacity. In production, throughput determines cost efficiency: higher throughput from the same GPU fleet means lower cost per token served.

attrs.alt: LLM throughput metrics taxonomy: request throughput, token throughput, cost efficiency, and bottleneck progression

content.text: Request throughput counts completed requests per second over a time window. It is the most intuitive metric — the load balancer and autoscaler both reason about it — but it conflates short and long requests, making it an unreliable proxy for GPU load.

A server processing 10 short (50-token) requests/s and one processing 10 long (2000-token) requests/s report the same request throughput but differ by 40× in GPU work. Always pair request throughput with token throughput when diagnosing bottlenecks.
— is the GPU-side efficiency metric that matters most for cost. It measures how many tokens the model generates per second aggregated across all concurrent requests.

Input (prompt) tokens also consume compute during prefill, but output tokens dominate cost in interactive serving because they determine how long each sequence occupies GPU memory and compute. The theoretical ceiling for output token throughput on a single H100×8 node for a 70B BF16 model:

attrs.latex: \text{Max tok/s} = \frac{\text{HBM bandwidth (B/s)}}{\text{model bytes per token load}} = \frac{3.35 \times 10^{12}}{140 \times 10^9} \approx 24 \text{ tok/s} \times \text{batch size}

content.text: At batch size 64 the theoretical ceiling is ~1,500 tok/s; practical throughput (accounting for KV cache reads, attention, and overhead) is typically 60–70% of theoretical, around 900–1,050 tok/s.
Cost efficiency converts throughput into business units. For cloud GPU deployments:

def cost_per_million_tokens(
    gpu_cost_per_hr: float,   # e.g. $32/hr for p4d.24xlarge (8×A100)
    output_tok_per_sec: float,
) -> float:
    tok_per_hr = output_tok_per_sec * 3600
    cost_per_tok = gpu_cost_per_hr / tok_per_hr
    return cost_per_tok * 1_000_000

# H100×8 on-demand ($98/hr), 1000 tok/s
print(cost_per_million_tokens(98, 1000))   # $27.2 / M output tokens

# A100×8 on-demand ($32/hr), 600 tok/s
print(cost_per_million_tokens(32, 600))    # $14.8 / M output tokens

# A100×8 spot ($9.6/hr), 600 tok/s
print(cost_per_million_tokens(9.6, 600))   # $4.4 / M output tokens

# Output tokens per second (primary throughput signal)
rate(vllm:generation_tokens_total[1m])

# Input tokens per second (prefill load)
rate(vllm:prompt_tokens_total[1m])

# Total tokens per second (input + output)
rate(vllm:generation_tokens_total[1m]) + rate(vllm:prompt_tokens_total[1m])

# Request completion rate
rate(vllm:request_success_total[1m])

# Running vs waiting requests (queue health)
vllm:num_requests_running
vllm:num_requests_waiting
— a non-zero value means the server is capacity-constrained and new requests are queuing. Pair with
to confirm the server is actively generating while the queue builds.

# GPU SM utilisation (compute saturation)
dcgm_fi_dev_gpu_util{gpu="0"}

# GPU memory utilisation
dcgm_fi_dev_mem_copy_util{gpu="0"}

# HBM bytes read per second (memory bandwidth)
rate(dcgm_fi_dev_fb_used[1m])

# Tensor core activity (indicates compute-bound workload)
dcgm_fi_dev_tensor_active

Correlate SM utilisation with token throughput: if SM utilisation is 40% and throughput is low, the bottleneck is memory bandwidth (decode is underutilising compute). If SM utilisation is 95% and throughput is still low, the bottleneck is compute (very large batch prefill or very small model).

# grafana-dashboard-snippet.yaml — key panels for throughput monitoring
panels:
  - title: "Output Token Throughput"
    type: timeseries
    targets:
      - expr: sum(rate(vllm:generation_tokens_total[1m])) by (model_name)
    fieldConfig:
      unit: "tok/s"
    thresholds:
      - value: 200
        color: red    # below this → scale out

  - title: "Queue Depth (waiting requests)"
    type: stat
    targets:
      - expr: sum(vllm:num_requests_waiting)
    thresholds:
      - value: 5
        color: yellow
      - value: 20
        color: red

  - title: "GPU SM Utilisation"
    type: timeseries
    targets:
      - expr: avg(dcgm_fi_dev_gpu_util) by (instance)
    fieldConfig:
      unit: "percent"
    thresholds:
      - value: 60
        color: green
      - value: 95
        color: red   # sustained 95%+ → thermal risk
LLM server throughput has four distinct operating regimes. Each has a different limiting factor and a different fix:
Large prompts consuming all compute in prefill
Chunked prefill; reduce max prompt length
Batch large enough to saturate tensor cores
Optimal — this is the target operating point
Decode loading weights each step; batch too small
KV cache full; sequences evicted and recomputed
The roofline model predicts whether a workload is compute-bound or memory-bound by comparing its arithmetic intensity to the hardware ridge point:

. Decode for a 70B model at batch 1: each token requires ~140 × 10⁹ weight bytes loaded for ~280 × 10⁹ FLOP — arithmetic intensity ≈ 2 FLOP/byte. Far below the ridge point, so decode is memory-bandwidth-bound at small batch. At batch 200+, the intensity rises above the ridge and transitions to compute-bound.

The roofline output above shows that for a 70B model on H100, memory bandwidth caps throughput below batch ~40. Above that point, compute becomes the limiter and throughput grows with batch size proportionally.
The most impactful lever for memory-bandwidth-bound workloads. Every doubling of batch size approximately doubles throughput up to the compute-bound regime. Set
in vLLM to the maximum concurrent sequences your GPU memory can hold:

# Calculate max batch given GPU memory budget
# Available KV cache = GPU_MEM * util - model_weights
# KV per token per layer = 2 * num_heads * head_dim * bytes (for K and V)

python3 -c "
gpu_mem_gb = 80        # A100 80GB
util = 0.90            # --gpu-memory-utilization
model_gb = 140         # 70B BF16
kv_bytes_per_tok = 2 * 64 * 128 * 2  # 2(KV) * heads * dim * bytes

available_bytes = (gpu_mem_gb * util - model_gb) * 1e9
max_kv_tokens = available_bytes / kv_bytes_per_tok
print(f'Available KV cache: {available_bytes/1e9:.1f} GB')
print(f'Max KV tokens:      {max_kv_tokens:,.0f}')
print(f'At max_seq=2048:    {int(max_kv_tokens/2048)} concurrent sequences')
"
# Available KV cache: 32.0 GB
# Max KV tokens:      32,768,000
# At max_seq=2048:    16000 concurrent sequences (if all 2048 tokens long)

TP across N GPUs splits the weight matrices, so each GPU loads only 1/N of the model per decode step. This reduces the per-GPU memory bandwidth pressure and can increase throughput — up to the point where inter-GPU communication overhead (NVLink all-reduce) starts to dominate:

For single-node TP (NVLink), 8-way TP is generally efficient. Across nodes (InfiniBand), TP communication overhead often makes TP-2 or TP-4 more cost-effective, with pipeline parallelism used for cross-node scaling.

vLLM's continuous batching dynamically adds new requests to the batch as slots free up from finished sequences. This eliminates the idle GPU cycles that static batching causes when waiting for a full batch:

# Static batching (TensorRT-LLM static mode, illustrative)
# GPU idle until batch_size requests are queued:
while True:
    batch = queue.get_batch(size=32)    # blocks until 32 requests arrive
    outputs = model.generate(batch)     # GPU active
    return outputs
    # GPU is idle during the blocking get_batch() call

# Continuous batching (vLLM default)
# GPU stays active: as one sequence finishes, a waiting request fills the slot
# No blocking wait — scheduler runs every decode step
Under bursty traffic, continuous batching keeps GPU utilisation 10–30% higher than static batching, directly translating to higher throughput without changing hardware.

groups:
- name: llm_throughput
  rules:
  - alert: LowOutputThroughput
    expr: |
      sum(rate(vllm:generation_tokens_total[2m])) < 200
    for: 5m
    labels:
      severity: warning
    annotations:
      summary: "Output throughput < 200 tok/s for 5 minutes"
      description: "Check queue depth and GPU utilisation. May need scale-out."

  - alert: HighQueueDepth
    expr: sum(vllm:num_requests_waiting) > 15
    for: 2m
    labels:
      severity: warning
    annotations:
      summary: "{{ $value }} requests waiting — potential throughput bottleneck"

  - alert: FrequentPreemptions
    expr: rate(vllm:num_preemptions_total[1m]) > 1
    for: 3m
    labels:
      severity: warning
    annotations:
      summary: "KV cache preemptions > 1/s — throughput will degrade"
      description: "Lower --max-num-seqs or reduce max_model_len"

apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: vllm-throughput-scaler
  namespace: llm-prod
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: vllm-deployment
  minReplicaCount: 1
  maxReplicaCount: 10
  cooldownPeriod: 120
  triggers:
  - type: prometheus
    metadata:
      serverAddress: http://prometheus:9090
      metricName: vllm_requests_waiting
      threshold: "10"        # scale out when >10 requests queued
      query: sum(vllm:num_requests_waiting)
  - type: prometheus
    metadata:
      serverAddress: http://prometheus:9090
      metricName: vllm_output_throughput
      threshold: "150"       # scale out when throughput drops below 150 tok/s per replica
      query: |
        sum(rate(vllm:generation_tokens_total[2m]))
          / count(kube_deployment_status_replicas_ready{deployment="vllm-deployment"})
Establish a throughput baseline before and after every configuration change. The vLLM benchmark script measures both throughput and latency under controlled Poisson arrival:

# Benchmark at 3 arrival rates to find the knee of the curve
for rate in 1 2 4 8 12; do
  python benchmarks/benchmark_serving.py \
    --backend openai \
    --base-url http://localhost:8000 \
    --model meta-llama/Llama-3-70b-instruct \
    --dataset-name sharegpt \
    --num-prompts 300 \
    --request-rate ${rate} \
    --output-json bench_rate_${rate}.json
done

# Parse results
python3 -c "
import json, glob
for f in sorted(glob.glob('bench_rate_*.json')):
    d = json.load(open(f))
    print(f'{f}: throughput={d["output_throughput"]:.0f} tok/s  '
          f'p95_ttft={d["p95_ttft_ms"]:.0f}ms  '
          f'p99_ttft={d["p99_ttft_ms"]:.0f}ms')
"
# bench_rate_1.json:  throughput=41  tok/s  p95_ttft=310ms  p99_ttft=420ms
# bench_rate_2.json:  throughput=82  tok/s  p95_ttft=340ms  p99_ttft=510ms
# bench_rate_4.json:  throughput=162 tok/s  p95_ttft=480ms  p99_ttft=790ms
# bench_rate_8.json:  throughput=287 tok/s  p95_ttft=1100ms p99_ttft=2800ms
# bench_rate_12.json: throughput=301 tok/s  p95_ttft=4200ms p99_ttft=9900ms  <- knee

The 'knee' in the throughput curve (where throughput flattens but latency spikes) identifies the practical capacity limit. In the example above, the knee is at ~8 req/s. Setting autoscaler triggers at 60–70% of this limit (≈5–6 req/s) gives headroom for burst traffic before latency SLOs are breached.
Maximising batch size and throughput pushes TTFT higher. Always track both simultaneously — a throughput gain that breaks your TTFT SLO is not a win.
Measuring throughput under synthetic load only:

Synthetic benchmarks with identical prompt lengths show 20–40% better throughput than production workloads with variable prompt and output lengths. Use production traffic replays for accurate capacity planning.
Confusing model throughput with API throughput:
vLLM's token counter measures model output tokens. Your API may add system prompt tokens, trim the response, or stream partial results. Track both model and API throughput separately.

High output throughput with low input throughput indicates very short prompts — often a sign the workload is synthetic or the system prompt is not being counted. Long prompts in production will hit a prefill bottleneck that the benchmark missed.
Single-node throughput numbers in multi-node specs:
H100×8 benchmark numbers do not scale linearly to multi-node. Inter-node NVLink or InfiniBand bandwidth caps TP efficiency. Always measure on the actual multi-node topology you'll deploy.

LLM throughput has three dimensions: request throughput (req/s) for autoscaling signals, output token throughput (tok/s) for GPU efficiency, and cost efficiency ($/M tokens) for business tracking. vLLM exposes all three via Prometheus counters. Throughput is memory-bandwidth-bound at small batch sizes and compute-bound above the roofline ridge point — increasing batch size via
is the primary optimisation lever for memory-bandwidth-bound deployments. Set KEDA autoscaler triggers at 60–70% of measured peak throughput to preserve TTFT SLOs during traffic bursts.
